original_abstract:
We introduce a novel family of ruthenium complexes defined by the general formula [Ruⁿ(tda)(py)₂]ᵐ⁺ (where n = 2, 3, 4), utilizing the pentadentate tda²⁻ ligand ([2,2':6',2"-terpyridine]-6,6"-dicarboxylate). Among these, the species [Ruᴵⱽ(OH)(tda-κ-N3O)(py)₂]⁺ (4H⁺), formed via hydroxo coordination to the precursor 3²⁺ under basic conditions, emerges as an extraordinary water oxidation catalyst. Characterization of these complexes was achieved through an integrated approach using X-ray diffraction, spectroscopic methods (NMR, EPR, UV-vis), and electrochemical techniques (CV, DPV, coulometry), supported by DFT calculations.

Structural analysis reveals that the coordination environment of the Ru center is highly sensitive to its oxidation state: the Ru(IV) species is seven-coordinated and diamagnetic, whereas the Ru(II) analogue is six-coordinated with a distinctive unbonded "dangling" carboxylate group. The Ru(III) state occupies a structural middle ground between these two geometries. We demonstrate that 4H⁺ can be generated from 3²⁺ either potentiometrically or voltammetrically, with both species coexisting in solution at specific pH ranges.

While the parent complex 3²⁺ remains catalytically inactive, 4H⁺ exhibits world-record performance. Foot of the wave analysis (FOWA) reveals unprecedented turnover frequencies (TOF) of 8,000 s⁻¹ at pH 7.0 and up to 50,000 s⁻¹ at pH 10.0. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations elucidate the full catalytic cycle, identifying a water nucleophilic attack on a Ru(V) oxo species as the rate-determining step. Crucially, the calculations highlight the functional role of the dangling carboxylate, which acts as an internal base. This intramolecular proton transfer significantly lowers the activation free energy for O–O bond formation, providing a blueprint for the design of high-efficiency molecular catalysts.
humanized_abstract:
A new family of ruthenium complexes described by the formula [Ruⁿ(tda)(py)₂]ᵐ⁺ has been synthesised and fully characterised (where n = 2, 3, 4 and tda²⁻ = [2,2':6',2"-terpyridine]-6,6"-dicarboxylate). Interestingly enough, the species [Ruᴵⱽ(OH)(tda-κ-N3O)(py)₂]⁺ (4H⁺) obtained by treating the precursor 3²⁺ with bases in aqueous solution proves to be an extraordinary water oxidation catalyst. Structural information on the complexes was obtained by using X-ray diffraction (XRD) methods as well as NMR, EPR and UV-vis spectroscopies. In addition, complementary experiments based on CV, DPV and coulometry, were carried out. DFT calculations supported these experimental results.

Structural analysis shows that the coordination environment around the Ru site varies as a function of its oxidation state; thus, the Ru(IV) complex is seven-coordinate and diamagnetic, while the Ru(II) analogue is six-coordinate with an unligated “dangling” carboxylate. The Ru(III) site occupies a structural regime intermediate between the Ru(IV) and Ru(II) sites. Reduction of the Ru²⁺ site at pH 10 with 3²⁺ affords 4H⁺, and this new proton is observed to be formed either at the electrode during voltammetric reduction or in the solution electrolyte under potentiostatic conditions within the pH range 7.5-10.5 and a specific current regime.

The parent complex 3²⁺ is catalytically inactive while 4H⁺ acts as a record-breaking catalyst. Foot of the wave analysis (FOWA) provides evidence of record-breaking turnover frequencies (TOF) of 8,000 s⁻¹ at pH 7.0 and up to 50,000 s⁻¹ at pH 10.0. The catalytic cycle for 4H⁺ is provided through a detailed density functional theory (DFT) study, indicating that the rate-determining step is water nucleophilic attack at the Ru(V) oxo. The DFT studies provide structural and kinetic insight into the catalytic cycle, demonstrating the critical role of the dangling carboxylate and confirming an intramolecular proton transfer that reduces the activation free energy for O–O bond formation, serving as a catalyst design strategy for highly active molecular catalysts. The parent complex 3²⁺ is catalytically inactive while 4H⁺ acts as a record-breaking catalyst. Foot of the wave analysis (FOWA) provides evidence of record-breaking turnover frequencies (TOF) of 8,000 s⁻¹ at pH 7.0 and up to 50,000 s⁻¹ at pH 10.0. The catalytic cycle for 4H⁺ is provided through a detailed density functional theory (DFT) study indicating that the rate-determining step is water nucleophilic attack at the Ru(V) oxo.
